verses.text: After seven silent days Job breaks out, cursing the day of his birth and wishing it swallowed in darkness. The dam of grief bursts at last. Even a saint may sink into despair, and God bears with the anguished words of a breaking heart.

After seven silent days Job breaks out, cursing the day of his birth and wishing it swallowed in darkness. The dam of grief bursts at last. Even a saint may sink into despair, and God bears with the anguished words of a breaking heart.

He longs that his birth-night had never been counted among the days, since it did not shut the womb against him. Sorrow makes him wish he had never been. Pain can blind even the godly to the mercy that gave them life.

Why, he asks, did he not die at birth and lie at rest with the kings and counsellors of the earth? He envies the quiet of the grave. To the crushed, death can look like the only peace, though God has better rest in store.

Why, he asks, did he not die at birth and lie at rest with the kings and counsellors of the earth? He envies the quiet of the grave. To the crushed, death can look like the only peace, though God has better rest in store.

There, he says, the weary are at rest, the prisoner free, the small and great alike, the servant loosed from his master. The grave levels every burden. Job sees only its release, not yet the hope beyond it that faith will recover.

Why is light given to the bitter in soul, who long for death more than for hidden treasure, whose way is hedged in by God? He cannot see why life is forced on the wretched. The mystery of suffering presses him hard, and he has no answer yet.

His sighing comes before his food, his roarings poured out like water, for 'the thing which I greatly feared is come upon me.' He has no rest, only trouble. The deepest dread has overtaken him, and faith must learn to trust where it cannot see.
